progressions.name: ii–V–I
progressions.summary: The fundamental jazz cadence. Every standard uses this.

progressions.name: minor ii–V–i
progressions.summary: Minor-key resolution. ii is half-diminished, V often altered.

progressions.name: I–vi–ii–V (rhythm changes)
progressions.summary: Classic turnaround. The A section of Rhythm Changes is built on this.

progressions.name: I–V⁷ pattern
progressions.summary: Tonic to dominant — the most fundamental harmonic motion.

progressions.name: I–IV–V⁷
progressions.summary: The 'three chord trick' that underpins folk, blues, and rock.

progressions.name: I–♭VII–IV–♭III
progressions.source: Weissman p.45 — keys of I and IV
progressions.summary: Used in 'On Broadway' and 'Lemon Tree'. The ♭VII gives a folk/rock color.

progressions.name: I–vi–IV–V (50s progression)
progressions.summary: The 'Heart and Soul' / 'Stand By Me' progression. Forever pop.

progressions.name: I–V–vi–IV (modern pop)
progressions.summary: The 'four chords' progression. Behind hundreds of pop hits.

progressions.name: Autumn Leaves (A section)
progressions.summary: Major ii-V-I to relative minor ii-V-i. The most-played jazz tune.

progressions.name: All The Things You Are (A1)
progressions.summary: Classic Kern modulation. Each phrase moves the tonal center.

progressions.name: Blue Bossa (A section)
progressions.summary: Minor blues with a brief modulation. Bossa nova feel.

progressions.name: Rhythm Changes (A section)
progressions.summary: The second most-played jazz form after the blues. AABA with bridge.
